key_points: Gravitation is the universal force of attraction between any two objects with mass
The universal law of gravitation states that force between two bodies is proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them
Gravitational constant (G) is a universal constant used in the gravitation formula
Free fall occurs when gravity is the only force acting on an object
Acceleration due to gravity (g) depends on Earth's mass and radius
The value of g decreases with increase in altitude and depth
Mass is constant everywhere but weight changes depending on gravity
Pressure exerted by solids depends on force and area of contact
Buoyant force depends on density of fluid and volume of fluid displaced
Archimedes' principle explains floating, sinking and apparent weight of objects
examples: Earth attracting objects toward its surface
Planets revolving around the Sun due to gravitational force
Camels walking easily on sand due to reduced pressure
Ships floating due to displacement of large volume of water
Submarines adjusting ballast tanks to control buoyancy
formulas: Acceleration due to gravity → g = GM/R²
Weight of object → W = m × g
Apparent weight → Actual weight − Buoyant force
exam_tips: Understand inverse square law for gravitational force
Remember that mass remains constant while weight varies with gravity
Practice numerical problems using W = mg
Use Archimedes' principle to solve buoyancy problems
Pressure decreases when area of contact increases
